title: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS)

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
index=proxy earliest=-24h
| stats sum(bytes_out) as upload_bytes dc(user) as users by dest_host
| lookup gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv saas_domain AS dest_host OUTPUT approval_status
| where isnull(approval_status) OR approval_status!="approved"
| where upload_bytes > 10485760
| sort - upload_bytes
| table dest_host, upload_bytes, users, approval_status
description: Personal data processed in unapproved SaaS breaks accountability and security measures. This use case identifies cloud applications from proxy, DNS, and CASB logs that are not in the approved catalogue — prioritising services with upload behaviour and OAuth grants to corporate identities.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes proxy. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain approved SaaS domain list with data residency notes; (2) baseline proxy categories; (3) alert on high upload to unknown SaaS; (4) correlate with O365 OAuth events for enterprise SSO abuse; (5) feed discoveries into ROPA gap analysis and enterprise architecture review. Indexes required: `index=proxy`; Lookups: `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog`, `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv`.
visualization: Table (unapproved SaaS), Bar chart (upload volume), Word cloud (domains — optional), Single value (new domains this week). Key fields: `approval_status`, `dest_host`, `upload_bytes`, `users`.
knownFalsePositives: PII detections from authorized data exports, finance reports, support cases with logged PII, or anonymized data testing.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=proxy`, DNS logs, `ms:o365:management` (OAuth consent), `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain approved SaaS domain list with data residency notes; (2) baseline proxy categories; (3) alert on high upload to unknown SaaS; (4) correlate with O365 OAuth events for enterprise SSO abuse; (5) feed discoveries into ROPA gap analysis and enterprise architecture review.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=proxy earliest=-24h
| stats sum(bytes_out) as upload_bytes dc(user) as users by dest_host
| lookup gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv saas_domain AS dest_host OUTPUT approval_status
| where isnull(approval_status) OR approval_status!="approved"
| where upload_bytes > 10485760
| sort - upload_bytes
| table dest_host, upload_bytes, users, approval_status
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS) (Art. 5(2), Art. 32)** — Personal data processed in unapproved SaaS breaks accountability and security measures. This use case identifies cloud applications from proxy, DNS, and CASB logs that are not in the approved catalogue — prioritising services with upload behaviour and OAuth grants to corporate identities.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=proxy`, DNS logs, `ms:o365:management` (OAuth consent), `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: proxy.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=proxy, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **dest_host**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(approval_status) OR approval_status!="approved"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Filters the current rows with `where upload_bytes > 10485760` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

This use case does not use a CIM tstats shortcut; the primary SPL above is the source of truth after field normalization in your environment.

Output fields: approval_status, dest_host, upload_bytes, users.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unapproved SaaS), Bar chart (upload volume), Word cloud (domains — optional), Single value (new domains this week).
